# Predicting monthly gross return using historical data 

**Introduction**  
The task is to predict the monthly gross return for each company at each month. Let i denote a company and t a month. The model maps a feature vector xi,t to a scalar target yi,t: yˆi,t \= f(xi,t), yi,t \= monthly gross returni,t. (1) This report focuses on the v3.0 and v3.1 pipelines, which represent the finalized and reproducible variants. Earlier prototypes (v1 and v2) are deprecated and excluded from evaluation. The design goals are:  Robust modeling for mixed-type, sparse tabular panels.  Strict chronological isolation for training, validation, and testing.  Residual correction to reduce systematic error without leakage.

**Understanding the data**  
The data given to us was in a tabular form, it contained monthly company level metrics and categories across multiple companies. The following are the fields giv![][image1]en in the data  
![][image2]

**A snapshot of the raw data is given below for reference**  
co\_code,Month,monthly\_gross\_return,mktcap,price,equity\_bv\_on\_stkdate,fy\_book\_value,lag\_mv,BM\_sep,Size\_Label,BM\_Label,Year,sa\_net\_worth,sa\_total\_assets,sa\_sales,sa\_cost\_of\_goods\_sold,sa\_total\_interest\_exp,sa\_selling\_distribution\_exp,Month\_annual,Corrected\_Year,Corrected\_Month,lagged\_book\_equity,lagged\_assets,lagged2\_assets,OpProf,Inv,OpProf\_Label,Inv\_Label,lag\_ret,log\_ret,Momentum,Mom\_Label

000241,2023-06,0.957575169983684,705.474,55.0,260.0,229.4,736.8997,0.2654041315281843,S,G,2023,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,0.9864188002930554,-0.013674267811346,0.1893310216435182,N

Naturally we started exploring architectures that can suit this kind of data while paying special attention to the categorical columns as we felt that it would be a powerful indicator of the monthly return.   
We found that tree based gradient boosting works well on tabular data however approaches like XGBoost struggle with making sense of categorical data. We found that CatBoost was a good option and viable to train locally on a cpu. 

**Data And Feature Engineering**  
We found that CatBoost performs ok but not well enough. So we decided to add another model to learn the residuals after catboost predicts the values.   
To be able to compare the different residual architectures the data was engineered the following way.   
1\. Panel identity: unique company-month mapping. 2\. Chronological order: all features respect time ordering. 3\. No future leakage: only data from τ ≤ t are used. 4\. Split integrity: train, validation, and test are disjoint in time. 5\. Deterministic canonicalization: duplicates resolved by fixed rules. 2.2 Cleaning, Imputation, and Target Scaling The data preparation protocol is consistent across v3.0 and v3.1:  Infinite suppression: ±∞ → NaN.  Missingness indicators: a binary flag is added per feature.  Median imputation: NaNs are filled by historical medians.

Then the following splits were made to train and evaluate our acitechtures   
![][image3]

These splits allowed us to ensure that there was no lookahead bias from the model and that the evaluation was fair across all candidate architectures. 

**Architecture**   
**Baseline:**   
CatBoost Regression : CatBoost fits an additive model of shallow decision trees using ordered boosting and ordered target statistics to reduce leakage for categorical fields. Additionally it is well matched to the dataset because it natively handles categorical features, missing values, and nonlinear interactions in tabular panels.

**Residual Models:**

1. CNN  
2. HMM : Hidden markov machine   
3. MLP  
4. Ridge residual   
5. Elastic net   
6. Transformer  
7. CatBoost residual

**Holdout Companies**  
We have taken companies 8, 27, 5048 and 5107 as holdouts. The models never see these companies during training. We later use these companies as a master evaluation to test the models performance for data that lies within the distribution
